5: wayyiḏrōš ʾĕlōhîm — 'he sought God' (2 Chronicles 26:5). The dārash root is the Chronicler's signature seeking-verb, appearing more often in Chronicles than in any other biblical book. The text specifies a teacher: Zechariah who instructed him in the fear of God — the seeking is not solitary but covenantally mediated. The result clause uses hiṣlîaḥ (to prosper, succeed): 'in the days of his seeking God, God prospered him.' The formula — seek God → prosper — is the positive side of the Chronicler's covenant calculus; its negative counterpart appears in the Ahaz account (2 Chronicles 28). The temporal marker 'in the days of his seeking' implies the seeking had a duration limit, anticipating 2 Chronicles 26:16's collapse.
16: wayyigbah libbô — 'his heart was proud' (2 Chronicles 26:16). The lēb + gābaH idiom is the Chronicler's standard formula for pride that precedes divine discipline: 'When he became strong, his heart rose up to his destruction.' The kᵉḥizzᵉqātô causal clause (when he became strong) shows the pattern: strength achieved through divine favor generates pride that overcomes divine fear. The same movement appears in Hezekiah (2 Chronicles 32:25) and Nebuchadnezzar (Daniel 5:20 uses the same ûgābaH libbō). The trespass (mā'al) consists specifically in crossing into the priestly domain — the sanctuary breach that triggers leprosy.
18: lōʾ-lᵉkā ʿuzzîyāhû lᵉhaqqṭîr laYHWH — 'it is not for you, Uzziah, to burn incense to the LORD' (2 Chronicles 26:18). The priest Azariah's rebuke deploys the name with pointed irony: ʿuzzîyāhû means 'YHWH is my strength' (ʿōz + YHWH), yet this man of YHWH-strength is being expelled from YHWH's sanctuary for overstepping the bounds that YHWH set. The phrase lōʾ-lᵉkā (not for you) marks the priestly prerogative claim — burning incense was reserved for the Aaronide priests (Num 16-17; Korah's rebellion involved the same boundary). The Chronicler's use of this story functions as a theological statement on the irreducibility of priestly vocation: political power does not authorize cultic function.
20: wayyibbāhᵃlûhû lāṣēʾt — 'they rushed/hurried him out' (2 Chronicles 26:20). The Piel of bhl (to hasten, panic) indicates the priests' alarm when they saw the leprosy appear on Uzziah's forehead (miṣraʿat bᵉmitsḥô). The Chronicler specifies gam-hûʾ niḏḥap lāṣēʾt — 'and he himself was in a hurry to go out' — suggesting the leprosy functioned as an immediate divine verdict that even Uzziah recognized. The noun miṣraʿat (leprosy/skin-disease) in cultic contexts is a sign of ritual exclusion; Miriam's affliction (Num 12) and Gehazi's (2 Kings 5:27) follow the same pattern of divine verdict through skin affliction.
1: wayyiqḥû kol-ʿam-yəhûḏāh ʾet-ʿuzziyyāhû — 'All the people of Judah took Uzziah' (2 Chronicles 26:1). The popular installation of Uzziah — 'all the people of Judah' (kol-ʿam-yəhûḏāh) — contrasts with contested successions elsewhere. The community's initiative in coronation parallels Josiah's installation (2 Chronicles 34:1) and Joash's (2 Chronicles 23:11), all cases where dynastic continuity is affirmed by the people. Uzziah was sixteen years old, meaning the people enthroned a teenager — a vote of confidence in the Davidic line after Amaziah's disgrace.
2: hûʾ bānāh ʾet-ʾElôt wayyəšîḇēhā lîhûḏāh ʾaḥărê šəḵāḇ hammelek ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw — 'He built Eloth and restored it to Judah after the king had slept with his ancestors' (2 Chronicles 26:2). Eloth/Elath (ʾElôt) on the Gulf of Aqaba is the Red Sea port that controlled trade routes to Arabia and Africa. Its recovery marks Judah's economic expansion southward. The detail that this happened 'after the king had slept with his ancestors' (after Amaziah's death) dates the achievement to early Uzziah's reign — his first act is recovering strategic territory, not court consolidation.
3: ben-šeš ʿeśrēh šānāh ʿuzziyyāhû bimlokô ûḥămiššîm ûštayim šānāh mālaḵ bîrûšālaim — 'Uzziah was sixteen years old when he became king, and he reigned fifty-two years in Jerusalem' (2 Chronicles 26:3). Fifty-two years is the second-longest reign in Judah after Manasseh's fifty-five (2 Chronicles 33:1), and the longest among the generally faithful kings. The Chronicler's theological point: Uzziah's long faithfulness (vv4-15) generates proportional longevity, before pride truncates his full functioning (vv16-21). The reign-length itself is commentary on covenant fidelity.
4: wayyaʿas hayyāšār bəʿênê YHWH kəkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśāh ʾăḇîhû ʾămatsyāhû — 'He did what was right in YHWH's eyes, just as his father Amaziah had done' (2 Chronicles 26:4). The comparative formula (kəkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśāh ʾăḇîhû) is the standard positive regnal evaluation. But the comparison to Amaziah is qualified praise: Amaziah did right 'but not with a complete heart' (2 Chronicles 25:2). Uzziah's reign will show both greater achievement than Amaziah's and the same ultimate failure pattern — success producing pride producing judgment.
6: wayyēṣēʾ wayyillāḥem bapəlištîm wayyipərōṣ ʾet-ḥômat Gaṯ wəʾēt ḥômat Yaḇneh wəʾēt ḥômat ʾAšdôḏ wayyiḇneh ʿārîm bəʾAšdôḏ ûḇapəlištîm — 'He went out and fought against the Philistines, broke down the walls of Gath, Jabneh, and Ashdod, and built cities in the territory of Ashdod and among the Philistines' (2 Chronicles 26:6). The Chronicler's military expansion account for Uzziah is far more detailed than 2 Kings 15:1-7, which gives only a one-verse summary of his reign. Three Philistine cities' walls demolished and Judean settlements built among them — the reversal of the Philistine pressure that characterized earlier periods (cf. 1 Sam).
7: wayyaʿzər hāʾĕlōhîm ʿālāyw ʿal-pəlištîm wəʿal-hāʿarāḇîm hayyōšəḇîm bəgûr-baʿal wəhammêʿûnîm — 'God helped him against the Philistines, against the Arabians living in Gur-baal, and against the Meunites' (2 Chronicles 26:7). wayyaʿzər hāʾĕlōhîm (God helped him) is the Chronicler's divine-assistance formula — the reward for dāraš-seeking (v5). The three enemy groups (Philistines, Gur-baal Arabians, Meunites) represent west, south, and southeast threats all neutralized under Uzziah's divinely empowered reign. This comprehensive geographic success is the Chronicler's evidence for covenant blessing.
8: wayyittənû hāʿammônîm minḥāh ləʿuzziyyāhû wayyēlek šəmô ʿaḏ-ləḇôʾ miṣrayim — 'The Ammonites brought tribute to Uzziah, and his fame spread as far as the entrance to Egypt' (2 Chronicles 26:8). Tribute from Ammon (minḥāh) marks Uzziah as a suzerain — the language of subordinate nations paying annual recognition. 'Fame spread to the entrance of Egypt' (wayyēlek šəmô) echoes Solomon's international reputation (2 Chronicles 9:23-24). The Chronicler presents Uzziah as a second Solomon in terms of geographic influence, before the fall that parallels Solomon's late-reign compromise.
9: wayyiḇen ʿuzziyyāhû miggdālôt bîrûšālaim ʿal-šáʿar happinnāh wəʿal-šáʿar haggayʾ wəʿal-hammiqṣôaʿ wayyəḥazzeqēm — 'Uzziah built towers in Jerusalem at the Corner Gate, the Valley Gate, and the angle of the wall, and he fortified them' (2 Chronicles 26:9). The three Jerusalem tower-locations — Corner Gate, Valley Gate, Angle — correspond to the northwestern and southwestern city walls. The Chronicler's detail on military infrastructure (towers, walls, cisterns, fortresses) shows Uzziah's administrative comprehensiveness: military, agricultural, and diplomatic expansion all narrated in vv6-15 as evidence of covenant blessing.
10: wayyiḇen miggdālôt bammidḇār wayyaḥṣōḇ bərôt rabbîm kî miqneh-raḇ hāyāh lô ûḇaššəpēlāh ûḇammîšôr ikārîm wəkōrəmîm bəhārîm ûḇakkarmel kî-ʾōhēḇ ʾădāmāh hāyāh — 'He also built towers in the wilderness and dug many cisterns, for he had large herds in the lowlands and on the plains; he also had farmers and vinedressers in the hills and fertile land, for he loved the soil' (2 Chronicles 26:10). ʾōhēḇ ʾădāmāh (a lover of the soil) — a unique characterization in Kings-Chronicles; Uzziah is presented as an agrarian king who personally values cultivation. The detail about cisterns (bərôt rabbîm) in the wilderness is practical: desert-water infrastructure enables herding and farming far from the settled zone.
11: wayhî-ləʿuzziyyāhû ḥayil ʿōśeh milḥāmāh yōṣəʾê ṣāḇāʾ ligdûḏ bəmispār pəqûḏḏātām bəyaḏ yəʿîʾēl hassôpēr ûmaʿăśêyāhû haššôṭēr — 'Uzziah had a trained army that went to war in units, according to the number enrolled by the secretary Jeiel and the officer Maaseiah' (2 Chronicles 26:11). The administrative detail — named secretary, named officer, registered units — reflects the Chronicler's interest in organized institutional religion and governance. The military bureaucracy (secretary + officer + commander Hananiah, v11b) mirrors the Levitical rosters of 1 Chronicles 23-26: good governance is structured, accountable, and documented.
12: kol-mispār rāʾšê hāʾāḇôt lagibbōrê ḥāyil ʾalpayim wəšēš mēʾôt — 'The total number of clan leaders commanding valiant troops was two thousand six hundred' (2 Chronicles 26:12). The precision of the military census (2,600 clan commanders) followed by the force total (307,500 in v13) demonstrates the Chronicler's interest in exact numbers as evidence of covenant blessing. The same precision applies to the Levitical rosters and the temple-dedication figures. Large, organized, precisely enumerated forces are the Chronicler's signature of a king walking faithfully with YHWH.
13: wəʿal yāḏām ḥêl milḥāmāh šəlōš-mēʾôt ʾelep ûšiḇʿat ʾălāpîm ûḥămiššē-mēʾôt — 'Under these commanders was an army of 307,500 men of great power' (2 Chronicles 26:13). bəkōaḥ ḥāyil (with mighty power) — the formula of military capability enabled by divine backing. The number 307,500 is the largest Judean army figure in Chronicles outside of Jehoshaphat's 1,160,000 (2 Chronicles 17:14-19). The Chronicler uses army size to index national spiritual health: large, disciplined armies correlate with covenant fidelity; reduced, chaotic forces with apostasy.
14: wayyāken lāhem ʿuzziyyāhû ləkol-haṣṣāḇāʾ māginnîm ûrəmāḥîm wəkôḇāʿîm wəširyōnōt wəqaššāṯôt wəʾaḇnê qelaʿ — 'Uzziah equipped the entire army with shields, spears, helmets, body armor, bows, and slings' (2 Chronicles 26:14). The comprehensive weapons list (six items) is the Chronicler's evidence of royal provision: kings who seek God equip their people. wayyāken (he provided/prepared) is the same root as David's preparations (kûn) for temple construction — royal preparation is a covenant virtue in Chronicles regardless of whether the object is temple or military equipment.
15: wayyaʿas bîrûšālaim ḥiššəḇônôt maḥăšeḇet ḥōšēḇ lihyôt ʿal-hammiggdālôt wəʿal-happinnôt lirəmôt ḥiṣṣîm ûləʾăḇānîm gədōlôt wayyēṣēʾ šəmô ʿaḏ-ləmērāḥôq kî hipliʾ laʿăzōr ʿaḏ kî-ḥāzāq — 'In Jerusalem he designed devices, invented by skilled craftsmen, to be placed on towers and corners to shoot arrows and hurl large stones. His fame spread far, for he was marvelously helped until he was strong' (2 Chronicles 26:15). hipliʾ laʿăzōr (marvelously/wondrously helped) — the Hiphil of pālāʾ (to be wonderful/extraordinary) applied to divine assistance. The very next word is the hinge: ʿaḏ kî-ḥāzāq (until he was strong) — and v16 begins: 'When he was strong (kəḥizqātô), his heart was lifted up.' Strength is the moment of greatest vulnerability.
17: wayyāḇōʾ ʾaḥărāyw ʿăzaryāhû hakkōhēn ûʿimmô kōhănîm laYHWH šəmônîm bənê-ḥāyil — 'The priest Azariah went in after him, along with eighty priests of the LORD — courageous men' (2 Chronicles 26:17). The priests are described as bənê-ḥāyil (men of valor/courage) — a military term applied to priestly resistance. Eighty is a significant number: enough to constitute a challenge by numbers, not an individual rebuke. The Aaronic priesthood's function as the sacred-space boundary-keepers requires courage; they literally stand between the king and the altar.
19: wayyizzāʿep ʿuzziyyāhû ûbəyāḏô hammiqṭeref ləqaṭṭēr ûkəzaʿpô ʿim-hakkōhănîm wəhaṣṣāraʿat zārəḥāh bəmiṣḥô — 'Uzziah, holding a censer, became furious. While he was raging at the priests, leprosy broke out on his forehead' (2 Chronicles 26:19). wayyizzāʿep (became furious) — the Hithpael of zaʿap, intense personal anger. The leprosy's outbreak precisely while he raged is the Chronicler's pointed synchronization: the divine judgment is simultaneous with the sin's expression. The forehead location (bəmiṣḥô) makes it visible to the priests — the physical mark appears at the exact moment of the confrontation.
21: wayhî ʿuzziyyāhû hammelek məṣōraʿ ʿaḏ-yôm môtô wayyēšeḇ bêt haḥopšît məṣōraʿ kî niggzar mibêt YHWH — 'Uzziah the king was a leper until the day of his death. He lived in a separate house (bêt haḥopšît) as a leper, for he was excluded (niggzar) from YHWH's house' (2 Chronicles 26:21). niggzar mibêt YHWH (cut off from YHWH's house) — the ultimate Chronicler consequence: access to the temple is the measure of covenant standing. The man who tried to force his way into the sanctuary dies cut off from it. bêt haḥopšît (house of freedom/separation) is a technical term for the royal leper's isolated residence (cf. 2 Kings 15:5).
22: wəyeter diḇrê ʿuzziyyāhû hārîʾšōnîm wəhāʾaḥărônîm kāṯaḇ yəšaʿyāhû ben-ʾāmôṣ hannāḇîʾ — 'The rest of the acts of Uzziah, from first to last, Isaiah the son of Amoz the prophet wrote' (2 Chronicles 26:22). The Chronicler uniquely names Isaiah as the historian of Uzziah's reign — the only place outside the book of Isaiah where Isaiah is named as a writer. Isaiah 1:1 dates his prophecy to the reigns of Uzziah through Hezekiah; the superscription confirms Isaiah was active during Uzziah's reign. The Chronicler's citation of Isaiah as source integrates prophetic literature into the regnal archive.
23: wayyiškaḇ ʿuzziyyāhû ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqbərû ʾōtô ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw biśdēh-haqqəḇûrāh ʾăšer lammləkîm kî ʾāmərû məṣōraʿ hûʾ — 'Uzziah slept with his ancestors and they buried him near his ancestors in the burial field belonging to the kings, for they said he was a leper' (2 Chronicles 26:23). The burial note is distinctive: not in the tombs of the kings but in the burial field 'belonging to the kings' (ʾăšer lammləkîm). The leprosy-exclusion applies even in death: he receives royal burial honor in proximity to but not identically with the Davidic tomb complex. The covenant consequences of his pride are permanent.
